title: Taiko Bridge SGX Enclave Key Leak Exploit

breakdown.note: Two transfers: 649,761.236201 (block 25368853, retryMessage) + 26,000.000260 (block 25368910, processMessage). Both from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA.
Internal transaction from Bridge proxy to attacker wallet 2 (0xa98035081fb739ebe9c8f80904668fb11438a846) at block 25368908 via processMessage. Price estimated at ~$2,400/ETH.

Block 25368955, processMessage, from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Token: 0x10Dea67478c5f8C5e2D90e5E9b26dBe60C54D800. Later sent to MEXC exchange via pass-through wallet 0x5FBc60A12bc6635e7d587d8dac52e4B1388b4990. Price estimated at ~$0.095/TAIKO (per PeckShield: 1.99M TAIKO = ~$189.12K).
From ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Token: 0xf939E0A03FB07F7171FF782c3FeA19166BA9F0E0. Verified via Etherscan tokentx API.
Block 25368854, retryMessage, from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Token: 0xdAC17F958D2ee523a2206206994597C13D831ec7.
Block 25368854, retryMessage, from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Attacker later withdrew WETH to native ETH via WETH.withdraw(). Price estimated at ~$2,400/ETH.
Block 25368855, retryMessage, from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Token: 0xD533a949740bb3306d119CC777fa900bA034cd52. Price estimated at ~$0.35/CRV.
Block 25368856, retryMessage, from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Token: 0x9Ad37205D608b8b219e6A2573f922094cec5C200. Price estimated at ~$0.015/iZi.
Block 25368855, retryMessage, from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Token: 0x2260FAC5E5542a773Aa44fBCfeDf7C193bc2C599. Price estimated at ~$65,000/WBTC.
Block 25368856, retryMessage, from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Token: 0xcd5FE23c85820f7b72d0926fc9b05b43e359b7ee. Price estimated at ~$2,600/weETH.

estimatedLoss.note: Total estimated loss ~$1.7M based on approximate token prices at time of incident (June 21, 2026). On-chain verified total across 10 asset types. Source estimates: PeckShield ~$1.7M, QuillAudits ~$1.7M, DeFi Nerd >$1M. Discrepancy: DeFi Nerd listed USDC as single 675,761.236201; on-chain shows 2 transfers totaling 675,761.236461 (0.000260 USDC rounding difference).

attackTime.note: Setup/proof batch at 2026-06-21T19:03:59Z (block 25367938). L1 asset releases from 2026-06-21T22:07:23Z (block 25368853) to 2026-06-21T22:28:11Z (block 25368955). Post-exploit fund movement continued to block 25369633. All timestamps verified from block.timestamp via eth_getBlockByNumber.

transactions.description: Phase 1 setup tx: Attacker EOA calls execute() (selector 0x09c5eabe) on executor contract 0xe0df6fc36deb38dd11dc53d327475c2b2b0ab98a. Registers 2 malicious SGX instances (IDs 5 and 6) via SgxVerifier.registerInstance() with instance keys 0x4777a54ff81357c966d7d98eea88f0f54d969135 and 0x292468e78605eca39071165b5eac4c9707ba9813. Saves forged Taiko checkpoint for block 1805600 (blockHash 0x69c9a97c034c4fd60be5dbd00cdad419acaf5ba30e6b8371437b7e919d1a49dd, stateRoot 0x5064e2a3dfde1cd6d164e07b5dae47433d20a2d2c1ba6c9bda60c1ab6765215f). Calls Bridge.processMessage() 10 times to set forged messages as RETRIABLE. Block 25367938, timestamp 2026-06-21T19:03:59Z. Status: SUCCESS. 24 logs emitted.

Second setup tx (ATTEMPTED BUT REVERTED, status=0x0): Attacker calls execute() (selector 0x09c5eabe) on executor contract at block 25368887. This transaction failed (gasUsed=4,117,088, 0 logs emitted). Likely a second attempt at SGX instance registration or checkpoint manipulation that reverted. Included for completeness of the attack timeline.

Drain tx 1: retryMessage() (selector 0x0432873c) on Bridge proxy 0xd60247c6848b7ca29eddf63aa924e53db6ddd8ec. Releases 649,761.236201 USDC from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Block 25368853, timestamp 2026-06-21T22:07:23Z. Status: SUCCESS.
Drain tx 2: retryMessage() on Bridge proxy. Block 25368853. Forged message release.
Drain tx 3: retryMessage() releases 138,139.564343 USDT and 20.70482 WETH from ERC20Vault. Block 25368854.
Drain tx 4: retryMessage() on Bridge proxy. Block 25368854. Forged message release.
Drain tx 5: retryMessage() releases 126,160.973069 CRV and 0.42634415 WBTC from ERC20Vault. Block 25368855.
Drain tx 6: retryMessage() on Bridge proxy. Block 25368855. Forged message release.
Drain tx 7: retryMessage() releases 2,140,403.026072 iZi and 0.530999 weETH from ERC20Vault. Block 25368856.
Drain tx 8: retryMessage() on Bridge proxy. Block 25368856. Forged message release.
Drain tx 9: processMessage() (selector 0x2035065e) on Bridge proxy. Internal transaction sends 130 ETH from Bridge to attacker wallet 2 (0xa98035081fb739ebe9c8f80904668fb11438a846). Block 25368908.
Drain tx 10: processMessage() releases 26,000.000260 USDC from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Block 25368910.
Drain tx 11: processMessage() releases 1,990,000 TAIKO from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA. Block 25368955, timestamp 2026-06-21T22:28:11Z.
Post-exploit: Attacker calls WETH.withdraw() (selector 0x2e1a7d4d) to convert 20.70482 WETH to native ETH. Block 25368937.
Post-exploit: Attacker transfers 1,990,000 TAIKO (selector 0xa9059cbb) to pass-through wallet 0x5FBc60A12bc6635e7d587d8dac52e4B1388b4990, which subsequently sent it to MEXC exchange. Block 25368959.
Post-exploit: 100 ETH sent to attacker wallet 2 (0xa98035081fb739ebe9c8f80904668fb11438a846). Block 25369404. First of 5 transactions splitting 500 ETH total.

attackers.description: Primary attacker EOA. Confirmed via eth_getCode (code_len=2 at both latest and attack block 25367938). Submitted all setup and drain transactions. Tx count: 149 at time of investigation. Received all ERC-20 drains from ERC20Vault. Swapped tokens via Uniswap V3 router (0x9108828e30f2de407aadb0af677b4a9228e4acd4), withdrew WETH to ETH, and transferred 1.99M TAIKO to MEXC via pass-through wallet.

Secondary attacker wallet. Confirmed via eth_getCode (code_len=2 at both latest and attack block). Received 130 ETH via internal transaction from Bridge proxy at block 25368908. Later received 500 ETH in 5x100 ETH transactions (blocks 25369404-25369633) from primary attacker EOA. Tx count: 3 at time of investigation (all inbound ETH transfers).

Pass-through wallet for TAIKO token laundering. Received 1,990,000 TAIKO from primary attacker EOA at block 25368959, then transferred same amount to MEXC exchange deposit address at block 25369002. Identified from PeckShield alert tweet image.

victims.description: Taiko Bridge Proxy contract. Confirmed via eth_getCode (code_len=342 at latest, code_len=4 at attack block indicating proxy). All 13 drain transactions (retryMessage and processMessage) targeted this contract. Released 130 ETH via internal transaction to attacker wallet 2.

Taiko ERC20Vault Proxy contract. Confirmed via eth_getCode (code_len=342 at both latest and attack block). All ERC-20 token drains (USDC, USDT, crvUSD, WETH, WBTC, weETH, CRV, iZi, TAIKO) originated from this contract. The vault was the custody sink, not the root cause of the bug.

Taiko SignalService Proxy contract. Confirmed via eth_getCode (code_len=342 at latest, code_len=4 at attack block). The attacker's setup tx called saveCheckpoint() on this contract to inject a forged Taiko block checkpoint (block 1805600), which was then used to make fabricated source signal proofs pass verification.

Executor contract called by attacker EOA via execute() (selector 0x09c5eabe). Confirmed via eth_getCode (code_len=4234 at latest, code_len=4 at attack block). This contract orchestrated the SGX instance registration, checkpoint saving, and message processing in a single batched transaction.

description:
On June 21, 2026, Taiko's L1 Bridge and ERC20Vault on Ethereum were exploited for approximately $1.7M through a forged SGX prover attestation attack. The root cause was an RSA-3072 private key (enclave-key.pem) used to sign all of Taiko's SGX enclaves, which had been committed to the public GitHub repository taikoxyz/raiko on a hotfix branch.

The attacker derived the MrSigner value (SHA-256 of the little-endian RSA modulus) from the leaked key and confirmed it matched the trustedUserMrSigner value stored on-chain in Taiko's L1 SgxVerifier contract. With this key, the attacker signed a malicious SGX enclave and registered it as a trusted prover instance via SgxVerifier.registerInstance(). The INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY was set to 0, allowing the malicious instances (IDs 5 and 6) to be immediately usable.

In the setup transaction (block 25367938, 2026-06-21T19:03:59Z), the attacker EOA 0x7506DeA0c38ca0B55364B22424374c5A1ae1B76a called execute() on the executor contract 0xe0df6fc36deb38dd11dc53d327475c2b2b0ab98a. This batched call: (1) registered 2 malicious SGX instances, (2) called SignalService.saveCheckpoint() to save a forged Taiko block 1805600 checkpoint with fabricated blockHash and stateRoot, and (3) called Bridge.processMessage() 10 times, each delegating to SignalService.proveSignalReceived() for source chain 167000, setting forged bridge messages to RETRIABLE status.

Approximately 3 hours later (blocks 25368853-25368955, 22:07:23Z to 22:28:11Z), the attacker executed 13 drain transactions: 8 retryMessage() calls and 5 processMessage() calls on the Bridge proxy. These released 10 different asset types from the ERC20Vault: 675,761 USDC, 138,140 USDT, 156,832 crvUSD, 130 ETH, 20.7 WETH, 0.43 WBTC, 0.53 weETH, 126,161 CRV, 2,140,403 iZi, and 1,990,000 TAIKO.

Post-exploit, the attacker swapped ERC-20 tokens to ETH via Uniswap V3 router (0x9108828e30f2de407aadb0af677b4a9228e4acd4), withdrew WETH to native ETH, sent 1.99M TAIKO to MEXC exchange via pass-through wallet 0x5FBc60A12bc6635e7d587d8dac52e4B1388b4990, and split 500 ETH to a second wallet (0xa98035081fb739ebe9c8f80904668fb11438a846) in 5x100 ETH transactions.

Taiko responded by pausing the L1 Bridge and ERC20Vault within hours. MEXC suspended TAIKO deposits. Taiko later replenished the bridge 1:1 from its own resources, confirmed no user lost funds, and brought the network back online on June 30, 2026. The bridge to Ethereum reopened with conservative withdrawal quotas.

On-chain verification (Phase 3): All 3 key transaction hashes verified via eth_getTransactionByHash (status=0x1 SUCCESS, selectors match expected functions, block timestamps match source claims). All 6 addresses verified via eth_getCode at both latest and attack block height (no EIP-7702 detected). Loss amounts verified by decoding Transfer events from 13 drain transaction receipts and cross-referencing with Etherscan tokentx API. 30 total attacker transactions in the attack window identified via Etherscan txlist API, including 15 drain/setup txs and 17 post-exploit consolidation/laundering txs.
rootCause:
The root cause was an operational key management failure: an RSA-3072 private key (enclave-key.pem) used to sign all of Taiko's SGX enclaves was committed to the public GitHub repository taikoxyz/raiko on a hotfix branch. This key was the entire trust root for Taiko's L2 state verification — MrSigner (SHA-256 of the little-endian RSA modulus) derived from this key was stored on-chain as trustedUserMrSigner in the SgxVerifier contract. Whoever holds this RSA private key can sign malicious enclaves that Taiko's L1 contracts will trust completely.

Two compounding contract-level design choices amplified the key leak: (1) processMessage() accepted any SGX attestation matching the registered MrSigner with no secondary signal (volume limits, second attestation source) to catch a state transition with no corresponding event on Taiko's actual L2; (2) retryMessage() trusted the RETRIABLE status set by a single prior forged attestation rather than re-verifying proof independently, converting one successful forgery into a repeatable drain primitive. Additionally, INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY was set to 0, allowing freshly registered SGX instances to sign proofs immediately with no cooling-off period.
attackVector:
1. Key acquisition: Attacker pulled enclave-key.pem from the public taikoxyz/raiko GitHub repo (hotfix branch, committed by user smtmfft in PR #197 'feat(raiko): Cherry-pick A7 updates(#182)').
2. MrSigner derivation: Attacker computed MrSigner = SHA-256(little-endian RSA modulus) from the leaked key and confirmed it matched trustedUserMrSigner on-chain.
3. Malicious enclave signing: Attacker built a custom SGX enclave and signed it with the leaked RSA key, producing a valid MrSigner match.
4. SGX instance registration: Attacker called execute() on the executor contract, which called SgxVerifier.registerInstance() twice (IDs 5 and 6). The crafted parameters passed DCAP verification (ECDSA signature verification + certificate chain validation). INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY=0 meant instances were immediately usable.
5. Checkpoint forgery: The same setup transaction called SignalService.saveCheckpoint() for Taiko block 1805600, injecting a fabricated blockHash (0x69c9a97c...) and stateRoot (0x5064e2a3...).
6. Forged message proof: Bridge.processMessage() was called 10 times, each delegating to SignalService.proveSignalReceived() for source chain 167000. The forged checkpoint root made fabricated Taiko-origin message signals pass Ethereum L1 verification, setting message status to RETRIABLE.
7. Asset drain: 8 retryMessage() and 5 processMessage() calls over ~20 minutes released 10 asset types from ERC20Vault and 130 ETH from Bridge. retryMessage() performed zero independent proof verification, trusting the RETRIABLE status set in step 6.
8. Fund laundering: Attacker swapped ERC-20 tokens via Uniswap V3, withdrew WETH to ETH, sent 1.99M TAIKO to MEXC via pass-through wallet, and split ETH across two wallets.
lessons: Never commit private keys, certificates, or .pem files to any Git branch — including hotfix branches that may bypass normal review. Implement automated pre-commit and pre-merge secret scanning in CI/CD.

A single RSA key controlling MrSigner for every enclave is a single point of total failure. Use multi-party enclave signing or implement key rotation and revocation paths that do not require a full contract upgrade.
retryMessage() must re-verify proofs independently rather than trusting a RETRIABLE status flag set earlier in the same trust chain. A status flag is not a proof.

INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY should be non-zero to provide a cooling-off period during which newly registered SGX instances cannot sign proofs, giving protocol teams time to detect and respond to malicious registrations.
processMessage() should implement secondary verification signals (volume limits, rate limits, second attestation source) to detect state transitions with no corresponding event on the source chain.

The SGX trust model assumes the signing key remains private. Once leaked, nothing distinguishes a legitimate enclave from an attacker-built enclave signed with the same key. Protocols should consider transitioning to cryptographic validity proofs (ZK proofs) that trust no enclave, no MrSigner, and no operator discipline.
AI tools can grep every commit of every public repo at machine speed. Assume that any secret committed to a public repo will be found. Treat repository hygiene as a security-critical practice.
